detailedExemplarData.title: Assessing the Impact of Germanyâs Renewable Energy Act in Promoting Renewable Energy

subject.title: Economics
subject.description:
**IB Economics** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **microeconomics, macroeconomics, the global economy, and development economics**, with a strong focus on **analytical and evaluative skills, real-world application of economic theory, and quantitative analysis**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Extended response questions | 1h 15min | 1h 30min | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Data response questions | 1h 45min | 1h 45min | 40% (SL), 30% (HL) |
| **Paper 3** | Mathematical questions | N/A | 1h | 20% (HL only) |
| **Internal Assessment** | Portfolio of 3 commentaries | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Topic and purpose are communicated accurately and effectively, with a tightly bounded focus on the EEG 2021 amendments and domestic renewable output.
Research question is clearly stated, focused and consistently connected to every section of the investigation.
Methodology employs an appropriate range of relevant sources and methods (Destatis, UBA, IEA, LCOE, cross-country comparison) with clear evidence of informed selection.
weaknesses: Minor ambiguity remains over whether âsuccessâ includes price stability in addition to output.
No explicit statement of criteria for selecting the 2020 baseline data set.
Introduction omits an explicit research gap statement to frame the novelty of the investigation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the descriptors for the top band (5â6): the topic and purpose are communicated with clarity and focus, the research question is sharply defined and integrated throughout, and the methodology is comprehensive and well justified. The minor issues noted (baseline choice, scope of âsuccessâ, research gap) are insufficient to withhold full marks in this criterion.

strengths: Source material (government statistics, industry reports, academic theory) is clearly relevant and appropriately applied to support key arguments.
Demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge of economic concepts (externalities, PPC, LCOE) and links them fluently to the German context.
Terminology is accurate and used consistently (feed-in tariff, cost-push inflation, capacity factor).
weaknesses: Occasional unsupported assertions (e.g., precise EEG contribution to job growth).
Conceptual error describing non-renewable resources as non-rivalrous.
Minor stylistic slip in terminology (âsuccessiveâ vs. âsuccessfulâ).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work displays a high level of knowledge and understanding across all strands: sources are applied with clear relevance, theoretical concepts are used coherently, and terminology is accurate. The few minor conceptual and stylistic lapses do not outweigh the overall strength, justifying the maximum mark in this criterion.

strengths: Research draws consistently on current, reputable and relevant data sets and literature, with effective triangulation between statistical agencies and academic sources.
Analysis is effective and well supported by evidence, integrating quantitative indicators (renewable share, LCOE) and economic diagrams to trace causal links.
Argumentation is well structured and includes critical evaluation of policy trade-offs, with a reasoned narrative linking micro and macro effects.
weaknesses: Depth of analysis is uneven in sections (e.g., LCOE assumptions and inflation shocks not fully disentangled).
Critical evaluation of counter-arguments (rebound effects, political feasibility) is only briefly noted rather than fully developed.
Assessment of source reliability is thin for some popular-media references.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation meets all descriptors for the highest band (10â12): research is consistently appropriate and relevant, analysis is effective with well-supported conclusions, and the argument is well structured with critical evaluation. The minor unevenness in depth and limited exploration of counter-arguments are outweighed by the overall excellence of critical thinking.

strengths: Follows the prescribed structure (title page, contents, introduction, conclusion, limitations, bibliography) with clear headings and sub-headings.
Page numbering, title and word count are present and correctly positioned.
weaknesses: Several entries in the table of contents lack page numbers, impeding navigation.
Duplicate section numbering (â4â) in the headings may confuse readers.
Some URLs break across lines and figures lack consistent source captions.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation is clearly appropriate overall (structure is well organized, required front matter is present), but persistent layout and formatting inconsistencies prevent full marks. The work sits in the upper part of the 1â2 band for layout, so a mid-band score of 3 out of 4 best reflects these strengths and weaknesses.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is stated verbatim on the title page and re-iterated in the introduction.  It is specific (EEG 2021, domestic production, 2021-24) and clearly connected to every subsequent sectionâdata sets, diagrams and evaluation all circle back to measuring the change in renewable output.  Focus is maintained throughout, with no significant drift into unrelated energy policy.  Hence the descriptor for a clearly stated, focused and effectively connected question is fully met.

The student formulates a tightly bounded investigation into the 2021 amendments of the EEG during 2021-24.  The title, accompanying overview and repeated sign-posting in the introduction make the focus and economic lens unmistakable.  The chosen period, policy and success-criterion (domestic renewable output) are all appropriate for systematic data collection and economic analysis.  Minor ambiguity remains over whether âsuccessâ also includes price stability, yet overall communication of topic and purpose is accurate and effective.

Government statistics, industry reports and academic concepts are woven through the narrative and diagrams.  Source material is generally well-matched to the points being made (e.g., Statista price series underpinning inflation analysis).  Occasional unsupported assertions (e.g., exact contribution of EEG to job growth) and repetition of secondary commentary limit complete appropriateness, so a solid but not exemplary 3-4 band is warranted.

The essay follows the prescribed EE structure with title page, contents, introduction, main body, conclusion, limitations and bibliography.  Headings and sub-headings facilitate navigation.  Minor sequencing issues (duplication of section 4) and occasional abrupt figure placement keep it from exemplary, but overall structure is clearly appropriate.

Page numbers, title and word count are present, and APA-style citations are attempted.  Nevertheless several URLs break across lines, some figures lack source captions, and equation formatting is inconsistent.  These lapses show âsomeâ but not full adherence to layout conventions, corresponding to the 1-2 band.

The student constructs a reasoned narrative linking negative externalities, subsidies and market outcomes, and offers some evaluation (short-run costs vs long-run benefits, comparison with France).  Evaluation, however, tends to be descriptive rather than critical; counter-arguments (e.g., rebound effects, political feasibility) are only briefly noted.  Organisation is clear but not âwell-structured throughout,â placing the work mid-way in the 4-6 band.

The analysis applies diagrams and quantitative indicators (renewable share, LCOE) to trace causal channels from policy tools to outcomes.  Arguments are generally logical; most conclusions (e.g., subsidy effect on supply) are supported by evidence.  Yet depth is uneven: the LCOE table rests on assumed parameters, and the inflation section acknowledges complexity but does not fully disentangle exogenous shocks.  Thus the essay merits the upper part of the 4-6 band.

The essay displays clear understanding of externalities, subsidies, PPCs and LCOE.  Explanations link theory to the German context with fluency.  Minor lapsesâsuch as confusing MPC/MSC labelling in the positive-externality diagram and limited treatment of price elasticityâprevent classification as consistently *coherent and sophisticated*, but knowledge is more than âpartially effectiveâ, placing it in the 3-4 band.

Most data sets and literature are current, reputable and directly relevant to the RQ.  The student triangulates between federal statistics, Statista, IEA and academic papers.  A few popular-media sources with limited authority are included, and the method for assessing reliability is thin, so the work falls short of the very top band but comfortably meets âmajority appropriate and clearly relevantâ.

The methodology outlines primary statistics (Destatis, UBA, IEA), media sources and IB economic theory, and it justifies tools such as LCOE and cross-country comparison.  Evidence of selection is present yet not always *effective*: reliability issues identified in Limitations are not anticipated in the Method section, and there is no explanation of criteria for choosing 2020 baseline or specific data sets.  Range is adequate rather than extensive, so the work fits best in the upper-mid (3-4) band.

Economic vocabulary (feed-in tariff, LCOE, cost-push inflation, capacity factor) is used accurately and consistently across 30+ pages.  Minor stylistic slips (e.g., âsuccessiveâ for âsuccessfulâ) occur but do not impair clarity.  Overall terminology use aligns with the descriptor for accurate and consistent application.

annotations.comment: The title succinctly conveys the focus on EEG 2021, timeframe and domestic renewable output, providing a clear research scope.
Including the word count is necessary, but the candidate code field remains blank. Ensure all administrative details are complete.
Several TOC entries lack page numbers, which impedes easy navigation. Include accurate page references.
Include explicit criteria for source selection (e.g., recency, credibility) and anticipated limitations.
The 2020 market data is described clearly, though referencing trends beyond 2020 would strengthen relevance.
Citation âMiloÅ¡, 2022â lacks full bibliographic detail; ensure all in-text references map to the bibliography.
The research question splits awkwardly across lines. Consolidate into one block for improved readability.
Table of contents is well structured, following the essay conventions with clear section headings.
Subsection numbering duplicates â4â in headings and sub-headings, which may confuse readers. Renumber to eliminate ambiguity.
The introduction provides strong context on Germanyâs targets but omits an explicit statement of the research gap.
The research question is clearly integrated within the introduction and guides subsequent sections effectively.
The introduction is heavy on policy background. Trim general context to sharpen focus on your specific investigation.
The hypothesis is concise and links EEG 2021 to renewable output and trade-offs, making it testable.
Terminology typo: the word âsuccessiveâ should be âsuccessfulâ to maintain accuracy.
Specify measurable criteria (e.g., % increase) in the hypothesis to tighten your research focus.
The methodology lists adequate sources but does not justify choice of 2020 as your baseline year.
Selection of government and NGO sources is well-reasoned, ensuring diverse perspectives on EEG impact.
Figure 1 caption is clear but omits source details; include full citation beneath the figure.
The discussion of deadweight loss is coherent and applies microeconomic theory effectively.
Quantify Q1 and Q2 using actual German output data to deepen the analysis.
Describing non-renewable resources as non-rivalrous is incorrect; they are rivalrous, making this a conceptual error.
In the positive externality diagram, MSC should lie below MPC; revise the diagram to reflect this.
Consider incorporating empirical examples of rebound effects or political feasibility to enrich evaluation.
Presentation of energy mix statistics demonstrates strong understanding of market context.
The positive externalities discussion draws on relevant theory to justify EEG intervention.
The narrative effectively links micro and macro effects, showing strong integration of theory with policy analysis.
